title: FINS3616 Week 6 Updated
body:
FINS3616 INTERNATIONAL BUSINESS FINANCE


                     LECTURE 6
       FOREIGN EXCHANGE DERIVATIVES


       Janghoon Shon (University of New South Wales)
 ILAB ASSIGNMENT

Format:             Moodle submission
Due:                Friday, July 18th, 17:00
% Evaluation:       15%
The task of the project is to forecast foreign exchange rates using regression models and to understand how to use
forecast information.
Each student is required to attend an iLab session in Week 3 or Week 4 in order to learn how to use the FACTSET
Database to access historical exchange rate data and macroeconomic information. Students are required to
download historical data from the FACTSET database and answer the list of assigned questions.
Each student will submit an individual assignment online through Moodle in Week 7. The specific details of the
project, as well as the specific assessment criteria, will be available on Moodle by Week 4. Students are also expected
to conduct their own research for this assignment.


                                                                                                                         2
 PLAN

W1: Introduction to International finance & exchange rates
W2: Description of FX markets & analysis of exchange rate risk
W3: International parity conditions
W4: Exchange rate systems and fundamentals
W5: Exchange rate trading and forecasting
    W6: Flexi Week
W7: Foreign currency derivatives
W8: FX risk hedging and management within multinationals
W9: International corporate issues
W10: International financing: cross-listing and Eurobonds

                                                                    3
 TODAY

1) Currency forwards and futures
      •   Characteristics of forward contracts
      •   Characteristics of futures contracts
      •   Margin requirements for futures contracts
      •   Difference in price between forwards and futures

2) Swaps in the FX market
      •   Characteristics of currency swaps
      •   Valuation of currency swaps
      •   Exchange rate (FX) swaps

3) Currency options
      •   Marketplace
      •   Using FX options
      •   Exercises


                                                                4
 CURRENCY DERIVATIVES MARKETS
 INTRODUCTION

Currency derivatives markets


Currency derivative turnover: 7.5 trillion USD (2022)
      •   Forward contracts         15%
      •   FX swaps                  51%
      •   Options                   4%




                                                           6
 INTRODUCTION

Currency derivatives markets




                               Source: Bank for International Settlements Triennial Central Bank Survey 2022, p.4
                               (https://www.bis.org/statistics/rpfx22_fx.pdf)                                          7

 INTRODUCTION

Derivatives markets (OTC, in US$ trillions)




                                                                                               11
                                              Data source: BIS OTC derivatives statistics
 INTRODUCTION

Derivatives markets (OTC, in US$ trillions)




                                                                                               12
                                              Data source: BIS OTC derivatives statistics
 FORWARD AND FUTURE CONTRACTS
 FORWARD AND FUTURE CONTRACTS

Forward and future contracts
Definition: A promise to buy or sell a specific quantity of a currency on a specific date.




                                                                                                14
 FORWARD AND FUTURE CONTRACTS

Characteristics of forward contracts
•   Private agreement between two parties (OTC)
     •   Terms of the contract specific to the needs
           •   Fixed maturities: 1, 2, 3, 6, or 12 months

     •   Large contracts
     •   Significant credit risk (counterparty risk), why?
     •   Main actors: large institutions
           •   Hedge funds, banks, multinationals, ...




                                                                15
 FORWARD AND FUTURE CONTRACTS

Characteristics of forward contracts
•   No quotes => no secondary market
      •   No liquidity
•   Fixed and non-reversible position, unless there is a mutual agreement between parties
•   No action until contract expiry
•   There is no margin call but a large initial deposit
•   Main use: to receive currency at a predetermined price.




                                                                                               16
 FORWARD AND FUTURE CONTRACTS




         Forward contract loss   Forward contract gain if
         if AUD/GBP < 1.98       AUD/GBP increases (i.e.
         (GBP depreciates)       GBP appreciates)              17
 FORWARD AND FUTURE CONTRACTS

Characteristics of future contracts
•   Contracts traded in an organized exchange
      •   Examples: CME (US), NYSE Liffe (Europe), TFX (Japan)
      •   See https://www.cmegroup.com/trading/why-futures/welcome-to-cme-fx-futures.html
•   Standardized contracts
      •   Small sizes (ex: CAD 100,000; EUR 125,000)
•   Fixed expiry dates (4x per year: Mar, Jun, Sep, Dec): IMM dates (the third Wednesday of the month)


      High daily liquidity, but not enough depth for a large player (e.g., >100 million USD position)




                                                                                                            18
 FORWARD AND FUTURE CONTRACTS

Characteristics of future contracts
•   The price is quoted, essentially, around the clock.
      •   Positions are "marked to market”
•   Easy to close a position before maturity
•   There is no credit risk; one must maintain a margin account.
      •   Guarantees to the counterparty the payment of future losses
      •   Risk of possible margin calls
•   Main use: hedging and short-term speculation
      •   The currency is rarely physically delivered/received




                                                                           19
 FORWARD AND FUTURE CONTRACTS

Margin deposits for future contracts
Initial Margin
•   Amount to be deposited when the contract is bought/sold
      •   Depends on exchange rate volatility
      •   USD 1000 to USD 4000 per contract on the CME
•   Example:
      •   https://www.cmegroup.com/markets/fx/g10/japanese-yen.html


•   Cash or collateral (e.g., government bonds)
      •   Alternatives: shares listed on the NYSE or physical gold
            •    Valuation at 70% of the current value

                                                                         20
 FORWARD AND FUTURE CONTRACTS

Margin deposits for future contracts
Maintenance margin
•   Minimum margin level required each day
     •   70% to 100% of the initial deposit level
     •   The client must cover his/her loss
           •   The contract can be closed if necessary




                                                            21
 FORWARD AND FUTURE CONTRACTS

Example of margin deposit for future contracts
Long a contract at t for December at 1.35 USD/EUR
     •   Contract size: 125 000 EUR
     •   Initial margin : 2000 USD
     •   Maintenance margin: 1500 USD



     What happens if at t+1 the rate changes to1.34 USD/EUR?
     •   Loss: 0.0100 USD per EUR ⇨ 1250 USD per contract
           •   @ t:     125 000 EUR x 1.35 USD/EUR = 168 750 USD
           •   @ t+1:   125 000 EUR x 1.34 USD/EUR = 167 500 USD

     •   Cash position 750 USD < maintenance margin 1500 USD
     ⇨ Margin call of 1250 USD

                                                                      22
 FORWARD AND FUTURE CONTRACTS

Valuation
The FX forward prices are derived from the covered interest rate parity (see session 1).


Additional features of the future contract:
     •   Opportunity cost if the initial margin deposit does not come from collateral (e.g., foregone interest)
     •   The contract is "marked to market".
            •   Margin call financing
            •   Uncertainty of amount and timing

     •   Liquidity to close/reduce position when needed
Despite these differences, forward and future prices (with the same maturity) are very similar, especially with transaction costs.



                                                                                                                                    23
 FORWARD AND FUTURE CONTRACTS

Main participants
•   Arbitragers
     •   Seek to earn risk-free profits by taking advantage of differences in interest rates among countries
     •   Use forward contracts to eliminate the exchange risk



•   Commercial traders/Hedgers
     •   Use forward contracts to eliminate or cover the risk of loss on export or import orders
     •   Mostly MNCs



•   Speculators
     •   Make profits from FX rate fluctuation


                                                                                                                  24
 SWAPS
 SWAPS

Currency swaps
•   Fixed-for-fixed currency swap
•   Exchange of interest payments and capital in one currency, to interest rate payments and capital into another currency.
•   Characteristics of swap contracts
      •   Private agreement between a client and a bank (OTC)
           •   Terms of the contract specific to individual needs
                 •   Long maturities (1 to 30 years)

           •   Large contracts

      •   Appear off-balance sheet
      •   No secondary market
      •   Reversible position only by mutual agreement.


                                                                                                                                 26
 SWAPS

Why use currency swaps?
•   Imagine an Australian firm having a USD 1 billion bond.
•   This firm would need to pay interest in USD every year.
      •   The amount becomes uncertain in AUD.
•   If the firm’s profits are mostly in AUD, it then faces FX risk.
•   Solution? Enter a USD for AUD currency swap with a bank.
•   It will “swap” the USD payments to fixed AUD payments!




                                                                         27
 SWAPS

Why use currency swaps?
•   Currency swaps can transform liabilities and assets
      •   Suppose that IBM can issue 15 million USD denominated bonds at 6%. The swap transforms this transaction into one
          where IBM has borrowed 10 million GBP at 5%.
      •   Reason: financing for specific project in the U.K. where future cash flows are expected to be in GBP. Match assets and
          liabilities.
      •   With a swap, the exchange rate risk is hedged.
•   Comparative Advantage
      •   Why did IBM not just borrow in the U.K.?
      •   Better credit history (more collateral) available in the U.S., hence credit spreads are lower for IBM if it borrows in the U.S.
      •   Sometimes, the bond market may be more liquid in USD.
      •   Especially the case for emerging markets
                                                                                                                                      28
 SWAPS

Illustrative example of a currency swap
•   5-year currency swap agreement between IBM and British Petroleum entered into on February 1, 2014
•   IBM pays a fixed rate of interest of 5% in GBP and receives a fixed rate of interest of 6% in dollars from BP.
•   The principal amounts are $15 million and £10 million.




                                                                                                                        29
 SWAPS

Characteristics of currency swaps
    •   A currency swap requires the principal to be specified in each of the two currencies (e.g., 15 million USD and 10 million
        GBP)
    •   The principal amounts are exchanged at the beginning and at the end of the life of the swap.
    •   Usually, the principal amounts are chosen to be approximately equivalent, using the exchange rate at the swap’s initiation.
    •   Zero value only at initiation!




                                                                                                                                     30
 SWAPS

Valuation of fixed-for-fixed currency swaps
•   The cash flows of a currency swap are equivalent to:
      •   Long position in fixed-rate bond in currency A.
      •   Short position in fixed-rate bond in currency B.




                                                                31
 SWAPS

Valuation of fixed-for-fixed currency swaps
Long bond in currency A




                                                                           Principal in currency A




                                                                       …

                                              Receipt of interest in currency A
                                                                                                        32
 SWAPS

Valuation of fixed-for-fixed currency swaps
Short bond in currency B




                                                                          Principal in B currency




                                                                      …


                                              Interest payment in B currency
                                                                                                       33
 SWAPS

Valuation of fixed-for-fixed currency swaps
Long bond in currency A + short bond in currency B = Currency Swap




                                                          Receipt of currency A



                                                                                  …


                                                          Payment of currency B
                                                                                         34
 SWAPS

Valuation of fixed-for-fixed currency swaps
Vswap is the value in currency A of an outstanding swap, where currency A is received and currency B is paid.
𝐵𝐴 is the value of the bond in currency A and 𝐵𝐵 is the value of the bond in currency B.
Then, we have


                                                 𝑉𝑠𝑤𝑎𝑝 = 𝐵𝐴 − 𝑆𝐴/𝐵 × 𝐵𝐵


Adjust the formula accordingly if, e.g., currency A is paid and currency B is received.




                                                                                                                   35
 SWAPS

Valuation of fixed-for-fixed currency swaps: example
•   Some time ago a financial institution has entered into a currency swap in which it receives 5% per annum in JPY and pays 8%
    per annum in USD once a year.
•   The principals in the two currencies are 10 million USD and 1,200 million JPY.
•   The swap will last for another 3 years.
•   The current exchange rate is JPY/USD = 110,
•   rJPY = 4% and rUSD = 9% for all maturities. The rates are continuously compounded
•   What is the USD value of the currency swap today?




                                                                                                                                 36
 SWAPS

Valuation of fixed-for-fixed currency swaps: example

           Time        Cash flow in USD Present Value in USD    Cash flow in JPY   Present Value in JPY
                   1               800,000         733,944.95           60,000,000        57,692,307.69
                   2               800,000         673,343.99           60,000,000        55,473,372.78
                   3               800,000         617,746.78           60,000,000        53,339,781.52
                   3           10,000,000        7,721,834.80       1,200,000,000      1,066,795,630.41
           Total                                 9,746,870.53                          1,233,301,092.40

                                   𝑉𝑠𝑤𝑎𝑝 = 1,233,301,092/110 − 9,746,871

                                           𝑉𝑠𝑤𝑎𝑝 = 1,464,958 USD



                                                                                                             37
 SWAPS

Other types of currency swaps
Examples
•   Fixed for floating currency swap
      •   A floating interest rate in one currency is exchanged for a fixed interest rate in another currency
      •   Valuation: combination of fixed-for-fixed currency swap and a vanilla interest rate swap in one of the currencies.
•   Floating for floating currency swap
      •   A floating interest rate in one currency is exchanged for a floating interest rate in another currency.
      •   Valuation: combination of fixed-for-fixed currency swap and a vanilla interest rate swap in both currencies.




                                                                                                                                  38
 SWAPS

FX swap (different from a currency swap!)
    1. Exchange USD for EUR at today's spot rate
    2. Promise to exchange EUR for USD in the future at the rate of the forward contract (pre-determined rate)
    3. Combination of Spot and Forward.




                                Exchange USD                         Exchange EUR
                                for EUR at spot                      for USD at the
                                rate                                 forward rate
                                                                                                                    39
 CURRENCY OPTIONS
 CURRENCY OPTIONS

Introduction to FX Options


What are FX Options?
 - FX Options give the holder the right, but not the obligation, to exchange money in one currency for another at a pre-agreed
exchange rate on or before a specified date.


Key Features:
  - Underlying Asset: Currency pairs (e.g., EUR/USD, GBP/JPY).
  - Types of Options: Call (right to buy) and Put (right to sell).
  - Premium: Cost of purchasing the option.
Used for hedging currency risk or speculation.

                                                                                                                                41
 CURRENCY OPTIONS

Main Types of FX Options
Vanilla Options:
  - Most common type, similar to standard options in other markets.
  - Call Option: Buy a currency pair at a specific price.
  - Put Option: Sell a currency pair at a specific price.
Exotic Options:
  - More complex with additional conditions.
  - Examples: Barrier options, Digital options, etc.
Important Terms:
  - Strike Price: Agreed exchange rate for the option.
  - Expiration Date: When the option can be exercised.

                                                                         42
 CURRENCY OPTIONS

Key Uses of FX Options
Hedging:
  - Corporations use FX options to hedge against unfavorable currency movements in international trade or investments.
  - Example: A U.S. company hedging against a fall in Euro.
Speculation:
  - Traders speculate on currency movements with options.
  - Example: Buying a Call option if you believe EUR/USD will rise.
Leverage:
  - Potential for high returns relative to the initial investment
Flexibility:
  - Can tailor strategies to different market views or needs.

                                                                                                                            43
 CURRENCY OPTIONS

Currency options: market
•   OTC options
     •   Generally European options, fixed strike ranges.
     •   Large amount, fixed horizon (e.g., 3 months), no exercise before maturity
•   Options traded on spot currencies
     •   Ex: Nasdaq PHLX for AUD, GBP, CAD, EU, CHF, NZD, JPY
•   Currency options on currency futures
     •   Ex: CME Group.
     •   See https://www.cmegroup.com/markets/fx/fx-options-on-futures.html




                                                                                        44
 CURRENCY OPTIONS

Currency options: definitions
•   Call options
     •   Right to buy a foreign currency (e.g., CHF) at a pre-determined rate (e.g., 0.9 USD/CHF)
     •   Amount to be paid fixed in reference currency (USD)


•   Put options
     •   Right to sell a foreign currency (e.g., CHF) at a pre-determined rate (e.g., 0.9 USD/CHF)
     •   Fixed receivable amount in reference currency (USD)




                                                                                                        45
 CURRENCY OPTIONS

Currency options: payoff at maturity
We have, if
•   the spot exchange rate at maturity is ST=USD/EUR
•   the exercise exchange rate is K=USD/EUR
•   the contract size is N EUR


Call options
•   Payoff in USD = Max(ST – K,0) x N


Put options
•   Payoff in USD = Max(K – ST ,0) x N

                                                          46
 CURRENCY OPTIONS

Currency options: payoff at maturity




                                          47
 CURRENCY OPTIONS

A Simple example
Scenario:
You expect the USD/EUR to rise from its current level of 1.10.


Action?
Buy a USD/EUR Call option with a strike price of 1.12, expiring in 3 months.


Outcome:
  - If USD/EUR rises above 1.12, you can buy Euros at the cheaper 1.12 rate than you can sell it back on the market.
  - If USD/EUR stays below 1.12, you can let the option expire, losing only the premium paid.



                                                                                                                          48
 CURRENCY OPTIONS

Use of currency options
Data call option on S=USD/EUR
     •   Price (premium) of the call option : c=0.03
     •   Exercise price: K=1.2
     •   Contract size: 10 000 EUR



Implications
Right to buy EUR with USD at a rate fixed by K=1.2 USD/EUR
     •   Purchase of EUR 10,000 for USD 12,000 (at time, t+1)
     •   Resale of 10000 EUR at spot @ St+1
     •   Cost: c = 0.03 USD per EUR of exposure

⇨ Gain/Loss: [max(St+1- K, 0) - c]x10000 EUR
                                                                   49
 CURRENCY OPTIONS

Use of currency options




                             50
                           Profit increases as spot rate S increases
CURRENCY OPTIONS          Max. loss is fixed = Option premium $1,250
                                                ($0.02/€ × €62,500)


Use of currency options




                                                                   51
                           Profit increases as spot rate S increases
CURRENCY OPTIONS          Max. loss is fixed = Option premium $1,250
                                                ($0.02/€ × €62,500)
                                            • 1,250 is the price of the option
                                            • You spend 1,250 to buy a “right”
Use of currency options                     • The max loss is that you don’t use
                                              the right.




                                                                        52
                           Profit increases as spot rate S increases
CURRENCY OPTIONS          Max. loss is fixed = Option premium $1,250
                                                ($0.02/€ × €62,500)
                                            • 1,250 is the price of the option
                                            • You spend 1,250 to buy a “right”
Use of currency options                     • The max loss is that you don’t use
                                              the right.




                                                                        53
                           Profit increases as t1 spot rate S
                          decreases
                          Max gain: $88,750 (when S = 0)
CURRENCY OPTIONS          Max. loss is fixed = Option premium
                          $1,250 = ($0.02/€ × €62,500)


Use of currency options




                                                               54
                           Profit increases as t1 spot rate S
                          decreases
                          Max gain: $88,750 (when S = 0)
CURRENCY OPTIONS          Max. loss is fixed = Option premium
                          $1,250 = ($0.02/€ × €62,500)


Use of currency options             • 1,250 is the price of the option
                                    • You spend 1,250 to buy a “right”
                                    • The max loss is that you don’t use
                                      the right.




                                                                     55
 CURRENCY OPTIONS

Use of options to reduce FX exposure
Let’s see an example where the use of options can help reduce an investor’s FX exposure


You want to invest in the European stock market:
•    The market Index today is at M0 = 1000 EUR
•    The spot exchange rate S0 = 1.5 USD/EUR



Imagine there are 2 cases in the future:
A)     MT = 1100 EUR & ST = 1.6 USD/EUR
B)     MT = 1000 EUR & ST = 1.2 USD/EUR



                                                                                             56
 CURRENCY OPTIONS

Use of options to reduce FX exposure
We can determine the future profits/losses of two different investment strategies:
•   Invest directly in the market by buying one unit of the market index: 1000 EUR x 1.5 USD/EUR = 1500 USD
•   Buy two ATM CALL index options for 30 USD/option


Let’s compare the two choices!




                                                                                                                 57
 CURRENCY OPTIONS

Value of a call option before maturity:



                                          Value if exercised today

                                          Higher spot price → higher
                                          value of call option




                                                                          58
 CURRENCY OPTIONS

Value of a call option before maturity:




                                          Higher time to maturity →
                                          higher value of call option




                                                                           59
 TUTOR SESSION: FX FUTURE, FORWARD, SWAPS, AND OPTIONS
